channel.title: Projects on Daniel Nwazue
channel.description: Recent content in Projects on Daniel Nwazue

item.title: Cane Stabilizer
item.description: This project was part of MIT's Assistive Technology (AT) design program, where student teams are paired with a real co-designer to build something that addresses a specific need in their life. Our co-designer was Susan Bibbins, a retired children's psychologist living with fibromyalgia and hemiparesis — conditions that leave her with one reliably functional hand. Susan uses a cane daily, and the problem she brought to us was simple but frustrating: when she sets her cane down, it falls over.

item.title: OX Truck Vibration Analysis
item.description:
As part of my engineering internship at OX Delivers in Leamington, England, I conducted a whole-body vibration (WBV) analysis comparing the OX4 truck's suspension performance against a Toyota Hilux — a well-established benchmark in the light commercial vehicle segment. The goal was to quantify driver and passenger vibration exposure across OX's test track routes and assess compliance with UK health and safety regulations.

Background

Whole-body vibration is a regulated occupational health risk in the UK under the Control of Vibration at Work Regulations 2005. Two thresholds govern how long a driver can be exposed before action is required:

item.title: 2.007 Robot
item.description:
2.007 (Design and Manufacturing I) is MIT's core mechanical engineering design course. Each student spends the semester designing and building their own robot to compete on a shared game board. The 2025 theme was based on the Wizard of Oz.

My goal: maximize points in both the autonomous and manual rounds. After analyzing the scoring breakdown, I zeroed in on two challenges — the train (38 pts × 2 laps = 76 pts) and the balloon (83.5 pts) — for a theoretical total of 159.5 points.

item.title: Smart Mill UROP
item.description:
The MIT Learning Engineering and Practice Group (LEAP), led by Dr. John Liu, is building the next generation of human-machine collaboration tools for manufacturing. Their flagship project — the Smart Mill — pairs a human operator with an intelligent robotic system mounted on a CNC vertical mill, with feedback delivered through extended reality (XR). I joined as a UROP researcher in Spring 2025, working directly under Dr. Rolando Bautista Montesano.

The Problem

Most human-machine collaboration systems today are built for simple, repetitive tasks. They rely on pre-programmed motions and can't adapt to real-time changes or interact dynamically with a human operator. This makes them ineffective for high-mix, low-volume manufacturing — exactly the kind of flexible, skilled work that still requires human judgment. The Smart Mill aims to change that by combining sensing, cognitive modeling, and XR feedback into a system that learns from expert operators and helps train new ones.

item.title: 2.008 Yo-Yo
item.description:
2.008 (Design and Manufacturing II) is MIT's production-scale manufacturing course. Rather than building one-off prototypes, each team designs a yo-yo and then manufactures 50 complete units using the same industrial processes found in real factories — injection molding, thermoforming, and CNC machining. The course is as much about process as it is about design.

This page documents the project as it unfolds. We're currently past the Design Review (Week 6) with molds machined and first injection molded parts in hand.

item.title: Small Projects
item.description:
E-Scooter Blind-Spot Detector

During MIT's IAP (Independent Activities Period) in January 2024, I received a $400 MIT grant to build anything I wanted. A friend had recently purchased an e-scooter, and after thinking about how exposed you feel on one — no mirrors, no awareness of what's behind you — I decided to build a real-time blind-spot detection system.

The system uses ultrasonic sensors (HC-SR04) mounted to a backpack, controlled by an Arduino Nano. I chose ultrasonic over infrared or LiDAR for the cost and reliability in noisy environments. It triggers two alert levels: a medium alarm when something comes within 10 feet, and a high-pitched alarm plus haptic vibration (via a Wii controller motor) when within 5 feet. I chose to mount the sensors to the rider instead of the scooter, to allow it to work across different vehicles.

item.title: Refrigerated Seed Dispenser
item.description:
In MIT's 2.00 Introductory Product Design course, our team was assigned the theme of "Gathering" and matched with volunteers from the Somerville Gardening Club and Somerville Growing Center — two organizations working to get more people to plant native, pollinator-friendly species instead of the decorative but ecologically barren plants typically sold at garden centers.

The Problem

The volunteers were already running community events and handing out seed packets, but they kept running into the same wall: many native Boston seeds require cold stratification before they'll germinate. These seeds naturally experience a cold winter period in the ground — without mimicking that process artificially, they simply won't sprout when planted in spring.

item.title: Boat Thruster Shield
item.description:
MIT Arcturus is a student team that competes in the annual RoboNation RoboBoat competition — an autonomous surface vehicle challenge. For the team's new flagship boat, someone needed to design a thruster shield: a structure that protects the thrusters from impact, mounts them securely to the hull, and can bear the full weight of the boat when it's resting on the ground between runs. I led that design.

The Problem

The boat uses an X-Drive thruster configuration — four thrusters mounted at 45° angles around the hull, which gives it omnidirectional maneuverability. That geometry made the shield design more constrained than a typical mount: each thruster needed to be angularly adjustable to dial in the exact X-Drive angles, while the overall assembly still had to be stiff enough to handle impact loads and support the boat's weight on land.
